SUNSET_GENERAL.rationale: f/8 provides a good foundation for overall scene sharpness with starburst potential on strong light sources.
SUNSET_GENERAL.proTip: Bracket 3 exposures (−1, 0, +1 EV) during rapidly changing golden hour colors for maximum flexibility in post.
SUNSET_GENERAL.commonMistake: Not setting compensation—cameras often underexpose sunsets by 1 stop. Apply +0.7 EV for balanced exposure.

SUNSET_BACKLIT_PORTRAIT.rationale: Expose for the subject's face. Warm backlight creates rim lighting and hair glow. Fill flash or reflector opens shadows.
SUNSET_BACKLIT_PORTRAIT.proTip: Use a collapsible reflector below the subject's face to bounce golden light upward—incredibly flattering.
SUNSET_BACKLIT_PORTRAIT.commonMistake: Letting the camera expose for the sunset silhouettes the subject against the beautiful sky.

SUNSET_SILHOUETTE.rationale: Expose for the bright sky only. Subject becomes a dramatic graphic element against the colored sky.
SUNSET_SILHOUETTE.proTip: Position subjects against the brightest part of the sky for maximum contrast silhouettes.
SUNSET_SILHOUETTE.commonMistake: Including distracting elements in the scene—silhouettes need clean, uncluttered backgrounds.

SUNSET_LANDSCAPE_TRIPOD.rationale: Tripod at 1/4s allows base ISO for maximum quality. f/16 maximizes landscape depth of field.
SUNSET_LANDSCAPE_TRIPOD.proTip: Shoot in RAW and bracket ±2 EV. Sunset skies change so fast you need multiple exposures to ensure the best outcome.
SUNSET_LANDSCAPE_TRIPOD.commonMistake: Not using a tripod means missing the sharp, high-quality golden hour shots that require slower shutters.

SUNSET_REFLECTIONS_WATER.rationale: f/11 keeps both the sky and water reflection in focus. 1/60s can be handheld with IBIS.
SUNSET_REFLECTIONS_WATER.proTip: A slightly slower shutter smooths out water ripples, creating a more mirror-like reflection in the water.
SUNSET_REFLECTIONS_WATER.commonMistake: Shooting from too high above the water—get down low to maximize the reflection in the frame.

GOLDEN_HOUR_STREET.rationale: Golden hour makes ordinary street scenes extraordinary. f/5.6 is versatile for multiple subjects at street distances.
GOLDEN_HOUR_STREET.proTip: Shoot into the sun to create flare and haze—ugly midday flare becomes magical in golden light.
GOLDEN_HOUR_STREET.commonMistake: Shooting only the sky and missing the golden light painting everything else in the scene.

GOLDEN_HOUR_ARCHITECTURE.rationale: Buildings glow warm in golden light. Tripod maintains sharpness during 1/60s architectural documentation.
GOLDEN_HOUR_ARCHITECTURE.proTip: Look for windows catching golden light and reflecting it back—these become beautiful secondary light sources.
GOLDEN_HOUR_ARCHITECTURE.commonMistake: Not using a tripod means losing sharpness in the building details at slower shutter speeds.

SUNSET_CREATIVE_EXPOSURE.rationale: 2-second exposure at sunset blurs cloud movement and smooths water for a dreamy, other-worldly quality.
SUNSET_CREATIVE_EXPOSURE.proTip: Use a 3-stop ND filter combined with f/22 at base ISO to achieve 2-second exposures during golden hour.
SUNSET_CREATIVE_EXPOSURE.commonMistake: Not using an ND filter—f/22 alone can't achieve 2s in golden hour without massively overexposing.

SUNSET_CLOUD_DETAIL.rationale: 1/125s freezes cloud structure while preserving detail in the highlights. f/11 renders texture across the whole sky.
SUNSET_CLOUD_DETAIL.proTip: Use graduated ND filter or HDR to balance bright sky against darker foreground elements.
SUNSET_CLOUD_DETAIL.commonMistake: Blowing the cloud highlights by basing exposure on the shadowed foreground—expose for the sky.

GOLDEN_HOUR_PORTRAIT_CLOSEUP.rationale: f/4 gives subject separation while keeping both eyes sharp in close-up. Warm golden light is universally flattering.
GOLDEN_HOUR_PORTRAIT_CLOSEUP.proTip: Have the subject close their eyes and then open them just before you fire—ensures open, relaxed eyes in the shot.
GOLDEN_HOUR_PORTRAIT_CLOSEUP.commonMistake: Not using eye detection—at f/4 in close-up, even a slight focus miss renders eyes soft.

SUNSET_LONG_EXPOSURE.rationale: 30-second exposure creates silky, ethereal water and cloud movement. Heavy ND filter essential in sunset light.
SUNSET_LONG_EXPOSURE.proTip: Use a 10-stop ND filter to achieve 30s exposures during vibrant sunset colors.
SUNSET_LONG_EXPOSURE.commonMistake: Not waiting for colors to develop—shoot through the whole sunset sequence as colors change every minute.

GOLDEN_HOUR_MACRO.rationale: Golden backlight through translucent petals and leaves creates magical macro results with a warm glow.
GOLDEN_HOUR_MACRO.proTip: Point macro lens directly toward the golden sun to use it as backlight—silhouette insects and translucent petals.
GOLDEN_HOUR_MACRO.commonMistake: Using golden hour for wide landscapes only—the extremely warm light at this time creates stunning macro results.

SUNSET_AERIAL_PHOTOGRAPHY.rationale: Aerial from helicopter or drone requires fast shutter to freeze vibration. Golden hour gives warm tones.
SUNSET_AERIAL_PHOTOGRAPHY.proTip: Remove horizon tilt post-process and use strong foreground-to-horizon diagonal lines to create depth.
SUNSET_AERIAL_PHOTOGRAPHY.commonMistake: Using slow shutter speeds from a moving helicopter creates motion blur in aerial shots.

SUNSET_BLACK_WHITE.rationale: Sunsets in B&W emphasize tonal contrast and graphic clouds against sky rather than warm color.
SUNSET_BLACK_WHITE.proTip: Increase the contrast and clarity in the red and orange channels during B&W conversion to make sunset clouds dramatic.
SUNSET_BLACK_WHITE.commonMistake: Expecting color sunset to translate well to B&W without tonal remapping—without color contrast is lost.

SUNSET_CITYSCAPE.rationale: City lights beginning to come on against sunset sky creates the magic moment of balance between day and night.
SUNSET_CITYSCAPE.proTip: Shoot in vertical orientation to capture foreground water reflection and sky with building details in one frame.
SUNSET_CITYSCAPE.commonMistake: Arriving at sunset instead of before it—setup takes time and the best light disappears quickly.
